caveats: a: OEWS combines furniture and other home-furnishings retailers and excludes self-employed owners.
a: Many retailers subcontract installation, so installer labor may sit outside payroll and the provided compensation ratio even though it remains operationally essential.
a: Generative-AI-affected hours include augmentation and better selling, not only substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: Technology interest does not prove implementation, continued use, or labor savings.
rho: The survey sponsor sells flooring business software and does not establish a probability sample.
rho: Errors in takeoffs, waste factors, product compatibility, or installation scope can erase savings.
e: The frozen LMM firm-count input is missing.
e: Survey respondents skew toward small independent software prospects and may not represent all firms.
e: A business dependent on the owner's estimating, customer relationships, or installer network may not be transferable despite being independent.
s5: Gallup and McKinsey are cross-industry sources.
s5: Trade-publication acquisition counts include commercial dealers and do not measure eligible firms at risk.
s5: Closure or sale of inventory and leases is not a qualifying transfer of continuing operations.
q: No source directly measures post-AI benefit retention.
q: Commercial and builder contracts may be more competitively bid and less retentive than residential remodel work.
q: Measurement or specification errors can turn apparent efficiency into warranty and rework cost.
d5: Legacy-code output is the closest direct time series but 2022 NAICS added portions of electronic and direct selling.
d5: Remodeling spending covers many project types and is nominal, while this primitive requires constant price and quality.
d5: Specialist-store quantity can diverge from total flooring demand as home centers and online channels gain share.
o: Only 41% of surveyed stores reported full-time employee installers, so accountable operation does not imply installation payroll.
o: Simple click-lock flooring and rugs are more self-serviceable than carpet, sheet goods, tile, or complex commercial work.
o: Big-box retailers can remain accountable operators while displacing LMM specialists.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based retail sales of flooring materials, frequently combined with measurement, estimating, delivery, installation, and repair; residential remodel is prominent, and operators generally quote a fixed project price with deposits and final payment tied to installation.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers and wholesalers without retail service, installation-only contractors outside the code, ceramic-tile-only or hardwood-only retailers classified elsewhere, captive chain units that cannot transfer independently, passive inventory or real-estate vehicles, and pure software marketplaces.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market floor-covering retailers that repeatedly serve external residential, builder, property-manager, or commercial customers through product selection, estimating, ordering, delivery, installation coordination, installation, repair, and after-sale accountability.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation mix for broader NAICS 449100, not wage-weighted task substitution in floor-covering retail. Floorzap surveys retail-flooring businesses and documents owner, sales, operations, office, warehouse, and installation roles but does not measure time or wages by task. Accenture's retail-wide affected-hours estimate includes augmentation. I map exposure to lead response, visualization, estimating support, proposal generation, marketing, CRM, purchasing, scheduling, invoicing, collections, and analysis, then discount physical samples, site measurement, substrate assessment, warehousing, delivery, cutting, installation, and repair. The broad BLS and large-retailer evidence may overstate exposure, while omission of owner labor may understate exposed management work.
caveats: OEWS combines furniture and other home-furnishings retailers and excludes self-employed owners.
Many retailers subcontract installation, so installer labor may sit outside payroll and the provided compensation ratio even though it remains operationally essential.
Generative-AI-affected hours include augmentation and better selling, not only substitution or avoided hiring.
a.change_evidence: A task-level time-and-wage study of independent floor-covering retailers using CRM, estimating, payroll, dispatch, warehouse, and installer records, with owner hours and already-automated tasks separately identified.
a.rationale: The broader BLS group has 49.40% sales employment, 12.73% office support, 6.92% management, and 3.44% design work, all with exposed cognitive components, but also 15.52% transportation/material-moving work and additional installation trades. The flooring survey shows owners commonly span sales, operations, office, warehouse, and installation roles, so substitution must be bounded below generic retail affected-hours claims.

d5.bridge: BLS/FRED measures real sectoral output for legacy NAICS 442210, the predecessor base of 449121, through 2025; Harvard forecasts total nominal homeowner remodeling spending only through early 2027, not five-year flooring quantity. I use the real output rebound from index 103.500 in 2023 to 110.111 in 2024 and 114.024 in 2025 as current momentum, then temper it with Harvard's projection of only 0.5% nominal remodeling growth by early 2027 and competition from non-specialist channels. This may overstate lens demand if big-box share rises or understate it if housing turnover and replacement cycles recover.
caveats: Legacy-code output is the closest direct time series but 2022 NAICS added portions of electronic and direct selling.
Remodeling spending covers many project types and is nominal, while this primitive requires constant price and quality.
Specialist-store quantity can diverge from total flooring demand as home centers and online channels gain share.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year US flooring unit forecast by material, project type, and channel, joined to housing turnover, housing-stock age, renovation permits, commercial vacancy/refurbishment, and inflation-adjusted NAICS 449121 sales.
d5.rationale: Floor covering is tied to replacement, renovation, property turnover, new construction, and commercial refurbishment. Real specialist-store output recovered in 2024-2025, while the near-term remodeling outlook points to stagnation below inflation. Aging housing supports replacement, but high rates, weak transactions, affordability, and big-box expansion constrain growth. Base-case five-year constant-quality quantity is approximately flat.

e.bridge: Floorzap reports that 90% of its survey respondents described themselves as independent retailers and 85% had one location, but this is a vendor-recruited survey, not the universe of LMM NAICS 449121 firms, and independence is not identical to eligibility. I discount for nontransferable owner dependence, franchise or buying-group restrictions, captive units, weak financial documentation, and operations outside the EBITDA lens. The survey likely overstates independence because independent dealers are its target users, but may understate multi-location LMM scale.
caveats: The frozen LMM firm-count input is missing.
Survey respondents skew toward small independent software prospects and may not represent all firms.
A business dependent on the owner's estimating, customer relationships, or installer network may not be transferable despite being independent.
e.change_evidence: A national firm registry matching NAICS 449121 establishments to beneficial ownership, store count, franchise/buying-group status, EBITDA, installation model, customer mix, and transferability of leases, contracts, and installer relationships.
e.rationale: The industry definition permits integrated installation and repair, creating operating businesses with customer relationships, staff or subcontractor networks, vendor terms, and local reputation. Industry evidence shows a predominantly small, independent channel and recurring acquisitions. Most LMM firms should be controllable going concerns, but the missing firm-count input prevents a target count.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[44]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Only 41% of surveyed stores reported full-time employee installers, so accountable operation does not imply installation payroll.
Simple click-lock flooring and rugs are more self-serviceable than carpet, sheet goods, tile, or complex commercial work.
Big-box retailers can remain accountable operators while displacing LMM specialists.
o.change_evidence: Project-level channel data separating DIY material-only, marketplace, home-center, specialist-retailer, and contractor-led sales, with the responsible party for measurement, installation, warranty, rework, and customer resolution.
o.rationale: Software can replace parts of inspiration, visualization, lead handling, simple takeoff, product comparison, ordering, and status communication. Most installed flooring still requires accountable scope definition, site measurement, substrate and moisture judgment, logistics, cutting, installation coordination, workmanship, warranty, and exception resolution. Even when installers are subcontracted, the retail operator remains accountable for combining product and project delivery. Pure material-only DIY and direct e-commerce form the displacement tail.

caveats: No source directly measures post-AI benefit retention.
Commercial and builder contracts may be more competitively bid and less retentive than residential remodel work.
Measurement or specification errors can turn apparent efficiency into warranty and rework cost.
q.change_evidence: Matched-project data before and after AI adoption covering quoted versus realized gross margin, material waste, change orders, installer rework, labor cost, project cycle time, win rate, prices for comparable scopes, and repeat/referral behavior.
q.rationale: Flooring is commonly sold as a locally quoted product-and-installation project, making total price less transparent than a simple shelf SKU and allowing early cost and throughput gains to remain with the operator. The industry survey reports wide but often substantial product margins, project deposits, and some direct pass-through of card costs. Big-box competition, bid comparison, builder contracts, buying-group pressure, and renewal repricing still share benefit with customers. The base retains somewhat more than generic product retail over five discounted years.

rho.bridge: The Floorzap survey measures current software use and stated 2025 interest rather than five-year realized implementation; respondents were mainly small independent flooring retailers and therefore closer to the lens than generic retail surveys. It reports interest in AI analytics and CRM but also limited e-commerce and manual or spreadsheet management among some firms. I translate those adoption signals into implementation of the exposed task pool, discounting fragmented catalogs, inaccurate measurements, field exceptions, weak data, integration cost, and change capacity. Vendor sponsorship may bias reported technology interest upward.
caveats: Technology interest does not prove implementation, continued use, or labor savings.
The survey sponsor sells flooring business software and does not establish a probability sample.
Errors in takeoffs, waste factors, product compatibility, or installation scope can erase savings.
rho.change_evidence: A follow-up cohort of surveyed flooring retailers verifying production AI deployments, integrations, error and rework rates, labor hours removed, adoption persistence, and realized payback by store revenue and employee count.
rho.rationale: AI-enabled visualization, lead triage, estimate drafting, waste checks, product matching, marketing, purchasing, scheduling, receivables, and management reporting can be deployed through vertical software within five years. Yet site measurements, installation judgment, legacy workflows, and the small scale of most stores make full implementation unlikely. Roughly half of exposed opportunity is the base case.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures planned sale or transfer across all US employer businesses, with 22% reporting five-year intent, while McKinsey shows most SMB exits are closures. Flooring trade sources document repeat acquisitions but no firm denominator and include commercial dealers as well as retailers. I discount stated intent for failed sales, closures, family reorganizations, and asset liquidations, while allowing that fragmented, long-tenured independent flooring firms may attract consolidators. The estimate could be high if owner dependence and installer shortages prevent sales or low if sector consolidation accelerates.
caveats: Gallup and McKinsey are cross-industry sources.
Trade-publication acquisition counts include commercial dealers and do not measure eligible firms at risk.
Closure or sale of inventory and leases is not a qualifying transfer of continuing operations.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year NAICS 449121 cohort linked to beneficial-owner changes, asset and stock sales, bankruptcies, closures, internal family transfers, and transaction values, segmented by owner age, EBITDA, residential/commercial mix, and installer model.
s5.rationale: A broad aging-owner pipeline and visible flooring-retailer consolidation support meaningful five-year transfer probability. Trade reporting identified six retailer acquisitions by one holding company by early 2024 and nine retail/commercial dealer acquisitions in 2025, but closures also occurred and observed deal flow lacks a population denominator.

principal_driver: Vertical software can improve estimating, visualization, scheduling, purchasing, marketing, and administration while integrated installation sustains an accountable local operator role.
principal_weakness: Physical field execution limits labor substitution, and housing sensitivity plus big-box expansion create wide uncertainty around durable specialist-channel demand.

sources.supports: Defines NAICS 449121 as retailers of new rugs, carpet, laminate, vinyl, linoleum, and most floor tile, including retailers combining product sales with installation and repair.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: Floor Covering Retailers

sources.supports: Reports the closest current occupation mix: sales 49.40%, office support 12.73%, management 6.92%, design 3.44%, transportation/material moving 15.52%, and carpet/floor/tile installers within the broader group.
sources.title: Furniture and Home Furnishings Retailers - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates

sources.supports: Surveys mainly small independent flooring retailers: 90% independent, 85% single-location, 81% residential-remodel focused, 41% with full-time employee installers, 12% with website e-commerce, and documents margins, payment terms, software adoption, and AI-analytics interest.
sources.title: 2025 State of the Retail Flooring Industry Report

sources.supports: Reports that 50% of retail working hours could be affected by generative AI and identifies customer, forecasting, inventory, supply-chain, and associate-assistance applications.
sources.title: Unleashing the Power of Generative AI in Retail

sources.supports: Reports that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, versus 9% of nonemployer owners.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Estimates more than half of US small-business owners are over 55 and six million firms will exit ownership over a decade; estimates 2022 SMB exits were 92% closures, 5% sales, and 3% transfers.
sources.title: Navigating the great small business ownership transition

sources.supports: Reports that 31st Street Capital had acquired six flooring retailers by early 2024 and planned another retail acquisition annually; also documents consolidation in flooring business-management software.
sources.title: Industry disrupters 2024: Internal factors, external forces upend status quo

sources.supports: Reports nine acquisitions in the retail and commercial dealer segment in 2025, an estimated 70 independent-retailer closures, and rapid store expansion by Floor & Decor, Home Depot, and Lowe's.
sources.title: Year in Review: 2025 Brought Another Year of Mediocre Activity Due to Uncertainty

sources.supports: Projects homeowner improvement and repair spending growth of only 0.5% by 2027 Q1, below inflation, with spending around $523 billion and stagnant interest tied to weak construction and housing.
sources.title: Remodeling Growth to Slow Sharply in Early 2027

sources.supports: BLS Industry Productivity series via FRED shows the real output index at 103.500 in 2023, 110.111 in 2024, and 114.024 in 2025 for the legacy floor-covering-store industry.
sources.title: Real Sectoral Output for Retail Trade: Floor Covering Stores (NAICS 442210)

title: Floor Covering Retailers
